items.q: Does the squeaky pop test detect oxygen?
items.correct: No — the pop with a lit splint is the test for hydrogen; oxygen relights a glowing splint instead.
items.misconception: Oxygen gives the squeaky pop because it supports burning.
items.random_wrong: The squeaky pop test detects carbon dioxide.
items.why: Both tests involve splints and burning, so students swap them; the pop is hydrogen exploding with oxygen from the air.

items.q: Why is dilute nitric acid added before the silver nitrate halide test?
items.correct: The acid removes carbonate ions, which would otherwise form a white silver carbonate precipitate and give a false positive.
items.misconception: The acid is there to dissolve the silver halide precipitate so it shows up better.
items.random_wrong: Hydrochloric acid is normally used because it makes the test stronger.
items.why: Students rarely think about interfering ions; note that using hydrochloric acid would itself add chloride and ruin the test.

items.q: Do Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ solutions give the same colour with sodium hydroxide?
items.correct: No — Fe²⁺ gives a green precipitate and Fe³⁺ gives a brown one, which is how the two iron ions are distinguished.
items.misconception: All iron solutions give the same rusty-brown precipitate with sodium hydroxide.
items.random_wrong: Iron ions give a blue precipitate just like copper ions.
items.why: Students think of 'iron' as one thing; the differing precipitate colours are precisely what reveal the ion's charge.

items.q: A lilac flame test colour indicates which ion?
items.why: Correct answer: Potassium. “Sodium” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The test for carbon dioxide is that it:
items.why: Correct answer: Turns limewater milky. “Pops with a lit splint” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Sodium hydroxide solution gives a blue precipitate with a solution containing:
items.why: It's Cu²⁺ ions. “Fe²⁺ ions” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Acidified silver nitrate gives a yellow precipitate with:
items.why: It's Iodide ions. “Chloride ions” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
